title: Introductions: Names, Origins, and First Questions
objectives: Introduce yourself naturally using me llamo, soy, and mi nombre es.
Ask someone's name formally and informally.
Say where you are from and where you live.
Choose between tu and usted in first conversations.
Continue a short introduction with y tu or y usted.

vocabulary.tip: Literally means I call myself. It is the safest everyday way to introduce your name.
Use soy for identity, name, origin, profession, and nationality.
Correct and clear, but more formal or official than me llamo.
Use with friends, peers, children, or relaxed first meetings.
Use with strangers, elders, clients, or anyone you want to address respectfully.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Good morning, what is your name?

vocabulary.tip: Use in casual conversation after names are exchanged.
Respectful version for formal introductions.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Excuse me, where are you from?

vocabulary.tip: Add a city, region, or country after de.
Use vivo en for where you live now, not necessarily where you are from.
Use after names are exchanged. It is natural across the Spanish-speaking world.
Use encantado if the speaker is male. Use encantada if the speaker is female.
Use encantada when the speaker is female.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: Pleased to meet you, I am Lucia.

vocabulary.tip: Use after answering to invite the other person to answer too.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I am from Canada. And you?

vocabulary.tip: Formal version of y tu.
vocabulary.exampleEnglish: I live in Winnipeg. And you?

vocabulary.tip: Use to add yourself to a statement.
Useful when saying you live here or are here with someone.
Use with de or en when talking about places.
Useful when talking about origin or nationality.
At A1, use trabajo to say I work.
At A1, use estudio to say I study.
Use soy de Canada for origin and vivo en Canada for residence.
A useful country name for conversations with Carlos.
Formal you. It uses forms like es and se llama.
Informal you. Use with peers and relaxed situations.
A natural reply to mucho gusto or encantado.

introductionPhrases.whenUsed: Me llamo is clear and safe. Soy plus your name is shorter and common in relaxed introductions. Encantado changes to encantada if the speaker is female.
Use mi nombre es in official, classroom, or very formal situations. In normal conversation, me llamo sounds warmer.
Use with peers, friends, children, or relaxed first meetings.
Use with strangers, elders, clients, and respectful introductions. The usted can be included for clarity or omitted when context is obvious.
Use after a greeting and name exchange in casual conversation.
Use in respectful or professional introductions.
Use soy de plus a city, region, or country to talk about origin.
Use vivo en for where you live now. It is different from soy de, which talks about origin.
Mucho gusto works everywhere. In Spain, encantado or encantada is also very natural.
Use informally after answering a name, origin, or residence question. Use y usted in formal situations.
Use formally after answering a question from someone you address with usted.

grammar.topic: Introducing yourself and keeping the first conversation going
grammar.explanation: Spanish introductions use short, flexible phrases. Start with a greeting from Lesson 1, add your name with me llamo or soy, then continue with origin, residence, or y tu / y usted.

notes.title: Me llamo vs mi nombre es
notes.text: Me llamo is the everyday introduction: Me llamo Tom. = My name is Tom. Mi nombre es Tom. is correct but more formal, like a form, presentation, or official setting. Practice: introduce yourself to Carlos with me llamo. Watch out: do not say Yo llamo Tom.

notes.title: Soy vs vivo
notes.text: Soy de says where you are from: Soy de Canada. = I am from Canada. Vivo en says where you live now: Vivo en Winnipeg. = I live in Winnipeg. Practice: say both your origin and where you live. Watch out: Soy en Winnipeg is not correct for residence.

notes.title: Tu vs usted
notes.text: Tu is informal and usted is formal. Tu eres de Mexico? = Are you from Mexico? Usted es de Mexico? = Are you from Mexico? Practice: ask Carlos informally, then ask an older person formally. Watch out: avoid tu with someone you want to address respectfully.

notes.title: Como te llamas vs como se llama
notes.text: Como te llamas? is informal: What is your name? Como se llama? is formal: What is your name? Practice: ask a friend, then ask a stranger. Watch out: como se llama can also mean What is his/her name? when you are talking about another person.

notes.title: De donde eres vs de donde es
notes.text: De donde eres? is informal: Where are you from? De donde es? is formal: Where are you from? Practice: ask Carlos where he is from and answer with soy de. Watch out: do not forget de in soy de Canada.

notes.title: Spanish often omits the subject
notes.text: Spanish usually does not need yo because the verb already shows I: Soy Tom. = I am Tom. Me llamo Tom. = My name is Tom. Practice: say three sentences without yo. Watch out: Yo soy Tom is correct, but overusing yo can sound heavy.

notes.title: Simple question formation
notes.text: Many A1 questions use the same word order as statements, with question intonation: Eres de Canada? = Are you from Canada? You can also use question words: De donde eres? = Where are you from? Practice: turn Soy de Canada into a question. Watch out: do not translate English word order word-for-word.

examples.spanish: Hola, me llamo Tom. Como te llamas?
examples.english: Hi, my name is Tom. What is your name?

examples.spanish: Soy de Canada, pero vivo en Winnipeg. Y usted?
examples.english: I am from Canada, but I live in Winnipeg. And you?

grammar.practicePrompt: Introduce yourself to Carlos, ask his name, say where you are from, say where you live, and return one question with y tu or y usted.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Hello, my name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Tom. I am Carlos.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Hi, what is your name?

lines.speaker: Ana
lines.english: My name is Ana. And you?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Buenos dias, mi nombre es Tom.
lines.english: Good morning, my name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Senora Ruiz
lines.english: Nice to meet you. What is your name?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Tom, esta es mi tia Ana.
lines.english: Tom, this is my aunt Ana.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Mucho gusto, Ana. Me llamo Tom.
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Ana. My name is Tom.

lines.speaker: Lucia
lines.english: Hi, I am Lucia. I work here.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Nice to meet you, Lucia. I am Tom.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Where are you from?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: I am from Canada. And you?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Carlos, where are you from?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Soy de Mexico, pero vivo aqui.
lines.english: I am from Mexico, but I live here.

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.english: Do you live in Canada?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.english: Yes, I live in Winnipeg.

lines.speaker: Senor Lopez
lines.english: I am from Mexico. And you?

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Soy de Canada y vivo en Winnipeg.
lines.english: I am from Canada and I live in Winnipeg.

lines.speaker: Learner
lines.spanish: Hola, me llamo Tom. Soy de Canada y vivo en Winnipeg. Como te llamas?
lines.english: Hi, my name is Tom. I am from Canada and I live in Winnipeg. What is your name?

lines.speaker: Carlos
lines.spanish: Me llamo Carlos. Soy de Mexico. Mucho gusto, Tom.
lines.english: My name is Carlos. I am from Mexico. Nice to meet you, Tom.

pronunciation.focus: Names, origin phrases, formal questions, and smooth linking.
tips: In many Latin American accents, ll in llamo sounds like English y.
Keep nombre as two clear parts: nom-bre. Do not add an extra vowel at the end.
Soy is one short syllable. Do not stretch it.
In de donde, keep de light and stress donde naturally.
Usted ends with a clear d sound, but it can be soft in natural speech.
In soy de, connect the words smoothly without stressing de.
Encantado and encantada stress ta: en-can-TA-do, en-can-TA-da.

items.note: Connect the words smoothly; many speakers say ll like y.
Keep nombre crisp; avoid saying nom-buh-ray.
One short syllable, with a clear oy sound.
Link soy de naturally; de is short and light.
Listen for de plus donde; do not drop de.
Practice the final d gently, not like a strong English d.
Link vivo en smoothly, but keep both words clear.

listeningPhrases.note: Listen for the smooth me llamo.
Notice that soy can introduce a name.
Listen for te, which makes it informal.
Listen for se, which makes it formal here.
Listen for de before the place.
Listen for vivo en, not soy de.

listeningPhrases.english: Hi, I am Tom. And you?
listeningPhrases.note: Natural connected speech with a follow-up question.

culture.title: Introductions set the tone
culture.text: Mucho gusto is natural right after you learn someone's name. Encantado or encantada means pleased to meet you and changes with the speaker: a male speaker says encantado, a female speaker says encantada. Use tu for informal situations and usted for respect, especially with older people, clients, or strangers. In many Spanish-speaking countries, people may use two surnames, usually one from each parent. Mi nombre es is correct, but it can sound more official than me llamo, which is warmer for everyday introductions.

nativeSpeech.title: How native speakers introduce themselves
notes: After hola from Lesson 1, many speakers go straight to soy plus their name in relaxed settings.
Me llamo remains completely natural and clear in first introductions.
Como se llama? often sounds more natural than always adding usted, as long as the formal context is clear.
People often answer origin with a city: Soy de Guadalajara, Soy de Winnipeg.
Y tu? and y usted? are simple ways to keep a first conversation from stopping.

commonMistakes.explanation: Me llamo literally means I call myself. The me is required.

commonMistakes.correction: Use soy de for origin and vivo en for where you live.
commonMistakes.explanation: Soy de Canada means I am from Canada. Vivo en Canada means I live in Canada.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using como se llama? with a friend when you mean informal you.
commonMistakes.correction: Use Como te llamas? with friends and peers.
commonMistakes.explanation: Como se llama? is formal when speaking directly to someone.

commonMistakes.mistake: Forgetting de in soy de Canada.
commonMistakes.explanation: Spanish needs de before the place of origin.

commonMistakes.mistake: Translating What is your name? word-for-word.
commonMistakes.correction: Say Como te llamas? or Como se llama?
commonMistakes.explanation: Spanish uses the idea What do you call yourself? for this question.

commonMistakes.correction: Say Soy Tom or Soy de Canada.
commonMistakes.explanation: Yo is correct, but Spanish often omits it because the verb already shows who is speaking.

review.summary: You can now introduce yourself, ask someone's name, say where you are from, say where you live, and keep the conversation going with y tu or y usted.
essentials: Name: Me llamo Tom. / Soy Tom.
Informal name question: Como te llamas?
Formal name question: Como se llama?
selfCheck: Can I introduce myself after saying hola?
Can I ask a name formally and informally?
Can I explain the difference between soy de and vivo en?
Can I return a question with y tu or y usted?
Can I speak to Carlos for 30 seconds using at least five lesson phrases?
review.nextStep: Now that you can introduce yourself, the next lesson will help you introduce the people closest to you.

quiz.prompt: What does me llamo mean?
Choose the natural casual way to say I am Tom.
How do you ask What is your name? informally?
What does mucho gusto mean?
Which phrase means I am from Canada?
Choose the formal way to ask What is your name?
What is the difference between soy de and vivo en?
options: Soy de is your name; vivo en is your job.
They mean exactly the same thing.
Soy de is origin; vivo en is where you live.
Vivo en is only for countries.
quiz.answer: Soy de is origin; vivo en is where you live.

quiz.prompt: A friend asks: De donde eres? Choose the best answer.
Which phrase is formal?
Choose the correct fill-in: Vivo _____ Winnipeg.
You meet Carlos' older neighbor. Choose the safest first question.
Choose the most natural short introduction after Hola.
Carlos says: Me llamo Carlos. Soy de Mexico. Choose the best response.
Which sentence avoids overusing yo?
options: Yo soy Tom y yo soy de Canada.
Soy Tom y soy de Canada.
quiz.answer: Soy Tom y soy de Canada.

quiz.prompt: Complete the conversation: Soy de Canada. _____?
Translate: Como te llamas?
Translate: Soy de Mexico.
Me _____ Tom.
Soy _____ Canada.
You want to return a question formally. What do you say?

speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Buenos dias, mi nombre es Tom. Mucho gusto.

speakingChallenge.prompt: Say where you are from and return the question informally.
Have a short introduction conversation with Carlos using at least five target phrases.
speakingChallenge.exampleAnswer: Hola, me llamo Tom. Soy de Canada y vivo en Winnipeg. Como te llamas? Mucho gusto, Carlos. Y tu?

realLifeMission.title: Real Life Mission
realLifeMission.mission: Introduce yourself to Carlos, ask his name, say where you are from and where you live, then return one question using y tu or y usted.
successCriteria: Use me llamo, soy, or mi nombre es with your name.
Ask Carlos his name with Como te llamas? or Como se llama?
Say where you are from with soy de.
Say where you live with vivo en.
Return one question with y tu or y usted.
